fields.brief_block: Verbal disagreement is not a "fighting words" situation under Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire, 315 U.S. 568 (1942), absent actual, face-to-face provocations of an immediate breach of the peace.
fields.opinion_block: These include the lewd and obscene, the profane, the libelous, and the insulting or “fighting” words — those which by their very utterance inflict injury or tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace. . . . The statute, as construed, does no more than prohibit the face-to-face words plainly likely to cause a breach of the peace by the addressee.
fields.finding_analysis: Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire is a First Amendment case defining the narrow "fighting words" exception to free speech, and it directly supports the brief's proposition. The Court limited the category to words "which by their very utterance inflict injury or tend to incite an immediate breach of the peace," and upheld the statute only as construed to reach "the face-to-face words plainly likely to cause a breach of the peace by the addressee." The brief's negative inference—that mere verbal disagreement, absent actual face-to-face provocation of an immediate breach of the peace, is not fighting words—follows directly from that definition, and the quoted phrase "fighting words" appears verbatim in the opinion.
